title: Juniper

infobox.value: are [coniferous](/wiki/Conifer "Conifer") trees and shrubs in the [genus](/wiki/Genus "Genus") ***Juniperus*** ([/dʒuːˈnɪpərəs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*joo-NIP-ər-əs*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))[[2]](#cite_note-2) of the cypress family [Cupressaceae](/wiki/Cupressaceae "Cupressaceae"). Depending on the [taxonomy](/wiki/Taxonomy "Taxonomy"), between 50 and 67 species of junipers are widely [distributed](/wiki/Species_distribution "Species distribution") throughout the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere") as far south as tropical [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), as far north as the [Arctic](/wiki/Arctic "Arctic"), and parts of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") and [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America").[*[not verified in body](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The highest-known juniper forest occurs at an altitude of 4,900 metres (16,100 ft) in southeastern [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet") and the northern [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas"), creating one of the highest [tree lines](/wiki/Tree_line "Tree line") on earth.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

**Junipers** are [coniferous](/wiki/Conifer "Conifer") trees and shrubs in the [genus](/wiki/Genus "Genus") ***Juniperus*** ([/dʒuːˈnɪpərəs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*joo-NIP-ər-əs*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))[[2]](#cite_note-2) of the cypress family [Cupressaceae](/wiki/Cupressaceae "Cupressaceae"). Depending on the [taxonomy](/wiki/Taxonomy "Taxonomy"), between 50 and 67 species of junipers are widely [distributed](/wiki/Species_distribution "Species distribution") throughout the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere") as far south as tropical [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), as far north as the [Arctic](/wiki/Arctic "Arctic"), and parts of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") and [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America").[*[not verified in body](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The highest-known juniper forest occurs at an altitude of 4,900 metres (16,100 ft) in southeastern [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet") and the northern [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas"), creating one of the highest [tree lines](/wiki/Tree_line "Tree line") on earth.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Juniper&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Juniperus_communis_cones.jpg/250px-Juniperus_communis_cones.jpg)](/wiki/File:Juniperus_communis_cones.jpg)

Cones and leaves of *[Juniperus communis](/wiki/Juniperus_communis "Juniperus communis")*

Junipers vary in size and shape from tall [trees](/wiki/Tree "Tree"), 20–40 metres (66–131 feet) tall, to columnar or low-spreading [shrubs](/wiki/Shrub "Shrub") with long, trailing branches. They are [evergreen](/wiki/Evergreen "Evergreen") with needle-like and/or scale-like [leaves](/wiki/Leaves "Leaves"). They can be either [monoecious](/wiki/Monoecious "Monoecious") or [dioecious](/wiki/Dioecious "Dioecious"). The female [seed cones](/wiki/Conifer_cone "Conifer cone") are very distinctive, with fleshy, [fruit](/wiki/Fruit "Fruit")-like coalescing scales which fuse together to form [a berrylike structure](/wiki/Juniper_berry "Juniper berry") ([galbulus](/wiki/Galbulus "Galbulus")), 4–27 millimetres (3⁄16–1+1⁄16 inches) long, with one to 12 unwinged, hard-shelled [seeds](/wiki/Seed "Seed"). In some species, these "berries" are red-brown or orange, but in most, they are blue; they are often aromatic and can be used as a [spice](/wiki/Spice "Spice"). The seed maturation time varies between species from 6 to 18 months after pollination. The male cones are similar to the other [Cupressaceae](/wiki/Cupressaceae "Cupressaceae"), with 6 to 20 scales.

In [hardiness zones](/wiki/Hardiness_zone "Hardiness zone") 7 through 10, junipers can bloom and release [pollen](/wiki/Pollen "Pollen") several times each year. Different junipers bloom in autumn, while most pollinate from early winter until late spring.[[4]](#cite_note-Ogren-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Jun_chin_close.jpg/250px-Jun_chin_close.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jun_chin_close.jpg)

Detail of *[Juniperus chinensis](/wiki/Juniperus_chinensis "Juniperus chinensis")* shoots, with juvenile (needle-like) leaves (left), adult scale leaves, and immature male cones (right)

Many junipers (e.g., *[J. chinensis](/wiki/J._chinensis "J. chinensis")*, *[J. virginiana](/wiki/Juniperus_virginiana "Juniperus virginiana")*) have two types of leaves; seedlings and some twigs of older trees have needle-like leaves 5–25 mm (3⁄16–1 in) long, on mature plants the leaves are overlapping like (mostly) tiny scales, measuring 2–4 mm (3⁄32–5⁄32 in). When juvenile foliage occurs on mature plants, it is most often found on shaded shoots, with adult foliage in full sunlight. Leaves on fast-growing 'whip' shoots are often intermediate between juvenile and adult.

In some species (e.g., *[J. communis](/wiki/J._communis "J. communis")*, *[J. squamata](/wiki/Juniperus_squamata "Juniperus squamata")*), all the foliage is of the juvenile needle-like type, with no scale leaves. In some of these (e.g., *J. communis*), the needles are jointed at the base, while in others (e.g., *J. squamata*), the needles merge smoothly with the stem.
The needle leaves of junipers are hard and sharp, making the juvenile foliage very prickly to handle. This can be a valuable identification feature in seedlings, as the otherwise very similar juvenile foliage of cypresses (*[Cupressus](/wiki/Cupressus "Cupressus"), [Chamaecyparis](/wiki/Chamaecyparis "Chamaecyparis")*) and other related genera are soft and not prickly.

Junipers are [gymnosperms](/wiki/Gymnosperm "Gymnosperm"), which means they have seeds, but no flowers or fruits. Depending on the species, the seeds they produce take 1–3 years to develop. The impermeable coat of the seed keeps water from getting in and protects the embryo when dispersed. It can also result in a long [dormancy](/wiki/Dormancy "Dormancy") that is usually broken by physically damaging the seed coat. Dispersal can occur from being swallowed whole by frugivores and mammals. The resistance of the [seed coat](/wiki/Seed-coat "Seed-coat") allows it to be passed down through the digestive system without being destroyed along the way. These seeds last a long time, as they can be dispersed long distances over the course of a few years.[[5]](#cite_note-:9-5)

### Sections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Juniper&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Sections")]

The genus has been divided into sections in somewhat different ways. A system based on [molecular phylogenetic](/wiki/Molecular_phylogenetics "Molecular phylogenetics") data from 2013 and earlier used three sections:[[6]](#cite_note-AdamSchw13-6)

* Section *Caryocedrus* – 1 species with large, blue, woody, 3-seeded cones; native to the Mediterranean
* Section *Juniperus* – 14 species with blue or red seed cones, often with 3 seeds; 12 species native to the [Eastern Hemisphere](/wiki/Eastern_Hemisphere "Eastern Hemisphere"), one endemic to North America, and one species, *J. communis*, circumboreal
* Section *Sabina* – about 60 species with variously coloured seed cones with 1 to 13 seeds; species about equally divided between the eastern and western hemispheres *Juniperus* sect. *Sabina* was further divided into clades.[[6]](#cite_note-AdamSchw13-6)

A new classification of gymnosperms published in 2022 recognised the sections as three separate genera: *Arceuthos* for section *Caryocedrus*, *Sabina* for section *Sabina*, and *Juniperus* *sensu stricto* for section *Juniperus*.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Ecology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Juniper&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Ecology")]

Juniper plants thrive in a variety of environments. The junipers from [Lahaul](/wiki/Lahaul_and_Spiti_district "Lahaul and Spiti district") valley can be found in dry, rocky locations planted in stony soils. Grazing animals and the villagers are rapidly using up these plants. There are several important features of the leaves and wood of this plant that cause villagers to cut down these trees and make use of them.[[14]](#cite_note-:0-14) Additionally, the [western juniper](/wiki/Juniperus_occidentalis "Juniperus occidentalis") plants, a particular species in the juniper genus, are found in [woodlands](/wiki/Woodland "Woodland") where there are large, open spaces. Junipers are known to encompass open areas so that they have more exposure to rainfall.[[5]](#cite_note-:9-5) Decreases in fires and a lack of livestock grazing are the two major causes of western juniper takeover. This invasion of junipers is driving changes in the environment. For instance, the ecosystem for other species previously living in the environment and farm animals has been compromised.[[15]](#cite_note-:2-15) When junipers increase in population, there is a decrease in woody species like mountain big [sagebrush](/wiki/Sagebrush "Sagebrush") and [aspen](/wiki/Aspen "Aspen"). Among the juniper trees themselves, there is increased competition, which results in a decrease in berry production.[[16]](#cite_note-:1-16) Herbaceous cover decreases, and junipers are often mistaken for weeds. As a result, several farmers have thinned the juniper trees or removed them completely. However, this reduction did not result in any significant difference on wildlife survival. Some small mammals found it advantageous to have thinner juniper trees, while cutting down the entire tree was not favorable.[[17]](#cite_note-:3-17)[[18]](#cite_note-:4-18)

Some junipers are susceptible to *[Gymnosporangium](/wiki/Gymnosporangium "Gymnosporangium")* rust disease and can be a serious problem for those people growing [apple](/wiki/Apple "Apple") trees, an alternate [host](/wiki/Host_(biology) "Host (biology)") of the disease.

Juniper is the exclusive food plant of the [larvae](/wiki/Larva "Larva") of some [moths and butterflies](/wiki/Lepidoptera "Lepidoptera"), including *[Bucculatrix inusitata](/wiki/Bucculatrix_inusitata "Bucculatrix inusitata")*, [juniper carpet](/wiki/Juniper_carpet "Juniper carpet"), *[Chionodes electella](/wiki/Chionodes_electella "Chionodes electella")*, *[Chionodes viduella](/wiki/Chionodes_viduella "Chionodes viduella")*, [juniper pug](/wiki/Juniper_pug "Juniper pug"), and [pine beauty](/wiki/Pine_beauty "Pine beauty"). Those of the [tortrix moth](/wiki/Tortrix_moth "Tortrix moth") *[Cydia duplicana](/wiki/Cydia_duplicana "Cydia duplicana")* feed on the bark around injuries or [canker](/wiki/Canker "Canker").

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Juniper&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Cultivation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Juniperus_%C3%97_pfitzeriana_%27Gold_Coast%27_03.jpg/250px-Juniperus_%C3%97_pfitzeriana_%27Gold_Coast%27_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Juniperus_%C3%97_pfitzeriana_%27Gold_Coast%27_03.jpg)

Junipers are among the most popular conifers to be cultivated as [ornamental](/wiki/Ornamental_plant "Ornamental plant") subjects for parks and gardens. They have been bred over many years to produce a wide range of forms, in terms of colour, shape and size. They include some of the dwarfest (miniature) cultivars. They are also used for [bonsai](/wiki/Bonsai "Bonsai"). Some species found in cultivation include:

## Toxicity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Juniper&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Toxicity")]

In drier areas, juniper pollen easily becomes airborne and can be inhaled into the lungs. This pollen can also irritate the skin and cause contact dermatitis.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Cross-allergenic reactions are common between juniper pollen and the pollen of all species of [cypress](/wiki/Cypress "Cypress").[[4]](#cite_note-Ogren-4)

Monoecious juniper plants are highly allergenic, with an [Ogren Plant Allergy Scale](/wiki/Ogren_Plant_Allergy_Scale "Ogren Plant Allergy Scale") (OPALS) rating of 9 out of 10.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Completely male juniper plants have an OPALS rating of 10,[[19]](#cite_note-OPALS-19) and release abundant amounts of pollen. Conversely, all-female juniper plants, totally lacking pollen, have an OPALS rating of 1,[[19]](#cite_note-OPALS-19) and are considered "allergy-fighting".[[4]](#cite_note-Ogren-4)

### Ethnic and herbal use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Juniper&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Ethnic and herbal use")]

Most species of juniper are flexible and have a high compression strength-to-weight ratio.[[20]](#cite_note-Hamm-20) This has made the wood a traditional choice for the construction of hunting bows among some of the [Native American cultures](/wiki/Indigenous_Peoples_of_North_America "Indigenous Peoples of North America") in the [Great Basin](/wiki/Great_Basin "Great Basin") region.[[21]](#cite_note-21) These bow staves are typically backed with sinew to provide tension strength that the wood may lack.[[20]](#cite_note-Hamm-20)

Ancient Mesopotamians believed that juniper oil could be used to ward off the [evil eye](/wiki/Evil_eye "Evil eye").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Embalming vessels in the burial chambers from a 26th Dynasty embalming workshop at Saqqara have shown the usage of Juniper oil/tar.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Some [Indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") use juniper in [traditional medicine](/wiki/Traditional_medicine "Traditional medicine"); for instance the [Dineh (Navajo)](/wiki/Navajo_people "Navajo people"), who use it for [diabetes](/wiki/Diabetes "Diabetes").[[24]](#cite_note-24) Juniper ash has also been historically consumed as a source of [calcium](/wiki/Calcium "Calcium") by the Navajo people.[[25]](#cite_note-:52-25)[[26]](#cite_note-:62-26)

Juniper is traditionally used in [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_culture "Scottish culture") folkloric and [Gaelic Polytheist](/wiki/Celtic_reconstructionism "Celtic reconstructionism") [saining](/wiki/Saining "Saining") rites, such as those performed at [Hogmanay](/wiki/Hogmanay "Hogmanay") ([New Year](/wiki/New_Year "New Year")), where the smoke of burning juniper, accompanied by traditional prayers and other customary rites, is used to cleanse, bless, and protect the household and its inhabitants.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Local people in [Lahaul Valley](/wiki/Lahaul_and_Spiti_district "Lahaul and Spiti district"), north India, present juniper leaves to their deities as a folk tradition. It is also useful as a folk remedy for pains and aches, as well as [epilepsy](/wiki/Epilepsy "Epilepsy") and [asthma](/wiki/Asthma "Asthma"). They are reported to collect large amounts of juniper leaves and wood for building and religious purposes.[[14]](#cite_note-:0-14)

### General use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Juniper&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: General use")]

Further information: [Juniper berry](/wiki/Juniper_berry "Juniper berry")

Juniper berries are a spice used in a wide variety of culinary dishes and are best known for the primary flavoring in [gin](/wiki/Gin "Gin")[[28]](#cite_note-28)(and responsible for gin's name, which is a shortening of the Dutch word for juniper, *jenever*). A juniper-based spirit is made by fermenting juniper berries and water to create a "wine" that is then distilled. This is often sold as a juniper brandy in eastern Europe. Juniper berries are also used as the primary flavor in the liquor [jenever](/wiki/Jenever "Jenever"). Juniper berry sauce is often a popular flavoring choice for [quail](/wiki/Quail "Quail"), [pheasant](/wiki/Pheasant "Pheasant"), [veal](/wiki/Veal "Veal"), [rabbit](/wiki/Rabbit "Rabbit"), [venison](/wiki/Venison "Venison"), and other game dishes.

A tea can be made from the young twigs.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Twigs or needles are used to flavour the traditional Finnish junperbeer, [sahti](/wiki/Sahti "Sahti") as well.

Dense and rot resistant, the irregular trunks of junipers have been used as fence posts and firewood. Stands that produce enough wood for specialty uses generally go under the common name "cedar",[[30]](#cite_note-:02-30) including *Juniperus virginiana*, the "red cedar" that is used widely in cedar drawers and closets.[[31]](#cite_note-mdc-31) The lack of space or a hyphen between the words "red" and "cedar" is sometimes used to indicate that this species is not a true cedar (*[Cedrus](/wiki/Cedrus "Cedrus")*).[[32]](#cite_note-reededu-32)

Juniper in weave is a traditional [cladding](/wiki/Cladding_(construction) "Cladding (construction)") technique used in Northern Europe, e.g., at [Havrå](/wiki/Havr%C3%A5 "Havrå"), Norway.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Juniper berries are [steam distilled](/wiki/Steam_distillation "Steam distillation") to produce an [essential oil](/wiki/Essential_oil "Essential oil") that may vary from colorless to yellow or pale green.[[34]](#cite_note-Cantrell-34) Some of its chemical components are [terpenoids](/wiki/Terpenoid "Terpenoid") and [aromatic compounds](/wiki/Aromaticity "Aromaticity"), such as [cadinene](/wiki/Cadinene "Cadinene"), a [sesquiterpene](/wiki/Sesquiterpene "Sesquiterpene").[[35]](#cite_note-35)
